## Step 1: Track your first change

Your team asks you to add a short welcome section to the handbook so new members can onboard quickly.

### 📖 Theory: Repositories and commits

<!-- GitHub-styled notifications can be used outside of ordered lists. Available options are: NOTE, IMPORTANT, WARNING, TIP, CAUTION -->
<!--
> [!NOTE]
> (Important note or additional information relevant to this section)
 -->

Version control records snapshots of changes over time so you can understand who changed what and when.

- A repository stores files and their history.
- A commit is a saved snapshot with a message that explains the change.
- Good commit messages describe intent, not just file names.

Read more:

- [About Git](https://docs.github.com/en/get-started/using-git/about-git)
- [Editing files](https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/working-with-files/managing-files/editing-files)

### ⌨️ Activity: Create and commit your first handbook update

1. Open the repository on the main branch.
1. Create a file named `handbook.md`.
1. Add a heading and a short welcome paragraph.

   Example:

   ```md
   # Team Handbook

   Welcome! This handbook introduces our team practices and onboarding process.
   ```

1. Commit the file directly to main with a clear message like `add handbook welcome section`.
1. Open the commit in the repository history and review the change.

<details>
<summary>Having trouble? 🤷</summary><br/>

- If you do not see the Add file option, refresh the repository page and verify you are on the main branch.
- Keep your commit message short and action-oriented.

</details>
